'''Shontayne Edward Hape''' ({{IPAc-en|ˈ|h|ɑː|p|eɪ}}; born 30 January 1981) is a New Zealand born former professional rugby footballer, a [[List of dual-code rugby internationals|dual-code rugby international]]. He played [[rugby union]] at centre for [[Montpellier Hérault Rugby|Montpellier]], [[London Irish]], [[Bath Rugby|Bath]] and [[England national rugby union team|England]]. He played [[rugby league]] for the [[Bradford Bulls]] in the [[Super League]] and the [[New Zealand Warriors]] in the [[National Rugby League]]. Hape represented [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]] at international level rugby league by being selected for both the junior and senior squads, including the [[2006 Tri-Nations]] [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]] squad. His usual position is {{rlp|ce}}. He was forced to retire in 2013 after suffering several serious concussions, which left him with decreased brain function.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www1.skysports.com/rugby-union/news/12321/9333990/shontayne-hape-confirms-retirement-from-rugby-and-calls-for-action-on-concussion|title=Shontayne Hape confirms retirement from rugby and calls for action on concussion|work=Sky Sports|date=1 June 2014}}</ref>

==Early life==
New zA [[Massey High School]] student, Hape started his rugby education as a [[Te Atatu Roosters]] junior, which was coached by his father and managed by his mother.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web |title=Match Fit Season 3 Ep 3 {{!}} SPORT {{!}} ThreeNow |url=https://www.threenow.co.nz/shows/match-fit/season-3-ep-3/S2339-580/M71150-618 |access-date=2023-05-03 |website=www.threenow.co.nz}}</ref> He played for the New Zealand Secondary Schools team in 1998.<ref name=jnrkiwis>''New Zealand Rugby League Annual '98'', [[New Zealand Rugby Football League]], 1998. p. 181</ref> Hape played for the [[Glenora Bears]] in the [[2001 Bartercard Cup]].

==Rugby league career==

===New Zealand Warriors===
Hape signed for the [[New Zealand Warriors|Auckland Warriors]] in 1999. In the same year, he played for the [[Junior Kiwis]] and [[New Zealand Māori rugby league team|New Zealand Māori]].<ref>Richard Becht [http://www.scoop.co.nz/stories/SP0003/S00018.htm Backs To The Future] ''Scoop Sport'', 16 March 2000</ref> In 2000 Hape made his [[National Rugby League|first grade]] debut for the Warriors and earned the great honour of Rookie of the Year award.

In 2001, the Warriors reached the play-offs and Hape played in the series. In round two of the 2002 season Hape injured his [[anterior cruciate ligament]], which ruled him out for the year and he missed the Grand Final.

===Bradford Bulls===
Fellow New Zealander [[Robbie Paul]] pointed out Hape to Bradford Bulls, who signed him in 2003. Helped to settle into English life by Paul and his wife, Hape missed only one of the Bulls' 35 games in the 2003 season, scoring 15 tries.

Hape played for the [[Bradford Bulls]] at centre and scored a [[Try (rugby)|try]] in their [[2003 Super League Grand Final]] victory against the [[Wigan Warriors]]. Having won [[Super League VIII]], Bradford played against 2003 NRL Premiers, the [[Penrith Panthers]] in the [[2004 World Club Challenge]]. Hape played at centre in the Bulls' 22–4 victory.

Hape played for the [[Bradford Bulls]] at centre and scored a [[Try (rugby)|try]] in their [[2004 Super League Grand Final]] loss against the [[Leeds Rhinos]]. A knee injury sustained while playing for [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]] in the [[2004 Tri Nations Series|2004 Tri Nations]] kept Hape out until round 22 in 2005. His return coincided with the Bulls charge towards the Grand Final and the Super League Championship. His partnership with [[Lesley Vainikolo]] is seen as one of the best in the game and has yielded many tries for the Bulls.{{citation needed|date=March 2011}} Hape played for the [[Bradford Bulls]] at centre in their [[2005 Super League Grand Final]] victory against [[Leeds Rhinos]]

Hape played for [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]] in their [[2005 Rugby League Tri-Nations]] Final victory over [[Australia national rugby league team|Australia]].
As [[Super League]] champions Bradford faced [[National Rugby League]] premiers [[Wests Tigers]] in the [[2006 World Club Challenge]]. Hape played at centre in the Bulls' 30–10 victory.
In March 2007, Hape was ruled out for most of the season because of a cruciate ligament injury. In August 2007, he was named in Bradford's [[Bradford Bulls#Team of the Century|team of the century]]. In September 2007, Hape returned for the round 27 Match v [[Hull F.C.]] His last game was against [[Hull Kingston Rovers]] on 29 June 2008.

===Representative career===
In 2004, Hape was chosen to represent New Zealand in the Gillette Tri-Nations, replacing his partner on the wing at the Bulls Vainikolo who was out with a knee injury. Hape played two Tri-Nations games and against France and also represented the [[New Zealand national rugby league team|Kiwis]] in the clash with Cumbria.

Hape also appeared for New Zealand in the Tri-Nations series in 2005, where he played a starring role helping New Zealand defeat the Aussies to win the final. In 2006, Hape played in the match against Great Britain in the one-off Test match at [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]].

==Rugby union career==

===Bath Rugby===
Hape qualified for the [[England national rugby union team|England team]] on residency grounds, having spent the last six years in England. After Bulls and New Zealand colleague Vainikolo made a successful switch to [[rugby union]] with [[Gloucester Rugby|Gloucester]], Hape was initially linked with [[Guinness Premiership]]-bound [[Northampton Saints]].<ref name="Hape considers union">{{cite web|url=http://www.sportinglife.com/rugbyleague/news/story_get.cgi?STORY_NAME=rleague/08/02/07/RUGBYL_Hape.html|title=Hape considers union|publisher=Sportinglife|date=7 February 2008|access-date=7 February 2008}}</ref><ref name="Union rumours won't distract Hape">{{cite web|url=http://www.thetelegraphandargus.co.uk/news/2024553.0/?act=complaint&cid=1104260|title=Union rumours won't distract Hape|publisher=Telegraph & Agrgus|date=7 February 2008|access-date=26 June 2008}}</ref>

In late May 2008, it was announced that he would be joining [[Bath Rugby]] on a three-year deal.<ref name="Bath set to sign Bulls star Hape">{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_union/my_club/bath/7419627.stm|title=Bath set to sign Bulls star Hape|publisher=BBC|date=26 May 2008|access-date=26 May 2008}}</ref>

===London Irish===
Coming to the end of his deal at Bath, the club and player were unable to come to an agreement over a new package. As a result, in February 2011 Hape agreed to join [[London Irish]] for the 2011–2012 season, replacing the departing Samoan centre [[Seilala Mapusua]].<ref>{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_union/my_club/london_irish/9404430.stm|work=BBC News|title=Exiles capture Bath centre Hape|date=22 February 2011}}</ref> After representing England in the World Cup in his native New Zealand, on his return he was cited for four weeks for a high tackle, followed by an injury plagued domestic season.

===Montpellier Hérault Rugby===
In April 2012 Hape terminated his contract with London Irish and signed with [[Montpellier Hérault Rugby]], and due to multiple  concussions in the 2012-2013 season, he was forced to retire and did not play for London Irish again.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-union/17784262|title = London Irish release centre Hape|work = BBC Sport}}</ref><ref name=":0" />

===Representative career===
On 13 January 2010, Hape was named in the [[England national rugby union team|English]] 32-man squad for the [[2010 Six Nations]] tournament.<ref name="NZ_Herald_10620169">{{cite news|url=http://www.nzherald.co.nz/sport/news/article.cfm?c_id=4&objectid=10620169&ref=rss|title=Former Kiwi to play rugby for England|date=14 January 2010|agency=[[Agence France-Presse|AFP]]|work=[[The New Zealand Herald]]|access-date=9 November 2011}}</ref>

On 30 May 2010, he played for England against the Barbarians at Twickenham.<ref>[https://newsimg.bbc.co.uk/sport1/hi/rugby_union/my_club/bath/8708011.stm England debut excites Bath's Shontayne Hape] ''BBC Sport Web Site '', 27 May 2010</ref> where he scored a try. He made his full Test debut for England two weeks later on 12 June in [[Subiaco Oval|Perth]] against [[Australia national rugby union team|Australia]] and appeared again a week later in Sydney in the 2nd Test of that English tour.

Hape was again selected for the 2011 England six nations squad and started in all five matches seemingly sealing his place as a dependable centre for England. Although there was fear that his pairing with Mike Tindall lacked the attacking power that other centres possessed however they made up for it with excellent defence leading to the England winning the tournament although narrowly missing out on the grand slam due to a defeat by Ireland.

Hape was later selected to play in the [[2011 Rugby World Cup]] in New Zealand, although there was doubt on the role he would play in the tournament due to the strong pairing of Mike Tindall and Manu Tulagi he did play in the 41–10 win over Georgia on 18 September scoring 2 tries in the first half.

==Personal life==
Hape is married to Liana, a dancer who has performed with [[Shaggy (musician)|Shaggy]], [[Pink (singer)|Pink]] and [[Natalie Imbruglia]]. The couple have three sons and one daughter,<ref name=BBC8457701>{{cite news|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_union/8457701.stm|title=Robbie Paul backs Shontayne Hape to be hit with England|publisher=BBC Sport|date=14 January 2010|access-date=14 January 2010}}</ref> with the youngest son being born after Shontayne retired.<ref name=":0" /> As of 2019, Shontayne Hape is a Business Development Manager for EWP Technical Solutions.<ref>[https://www.linkedin.com/in/shontaynehape Linkedin.com: Shontayne Hape]</ref>

His pastime is [[DJing]], which is influenced by rap artists such as [[Jay-Z]] and [[Kanye West]]. He undertakes under the stage name '''DJ Shape.''' Hape performed in Bath in 2009 to assist those affected by the 2009 tsunami that hit Samoa.<ref name=BBC8457701/>

In 2023, Hape participated in season 3 of ''Match Fit'', where former rugby league players return to play against the Australian counterparts. He joined in the first season that featured former [[rugby league]] stars.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Match Fit Season 3 Ep 1 {{!}} SPORT {{!}} ThreeNow |url=https://www.threenow.co.nz/shows/match-fit/season-3-ep-1/S2339-580/M70717-275 |access-date=2023-04-13 |website=www.threenow.co.nz}}</ref> He revealed in episode 3 that he suffered his first major concussion when he was 9, but knowledge of concussion, [[Chronic traumatic encephalopathy|CTE]] and repetitive head trauma was minimal at the time. Owing to this, Dr [[John Mayhew (doctor)|John Mayhew]] advised him against playing contact sports, making him the second person since [[Ron Cribb|Rob Cribb]] in season 1 to be medically barred from playing contact sports.<ref name=":0" />

In 2024, Hape returned to ''Match Fit: Union vs. League'' only as a guest so he can deal with his mental struggles away from the programme.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Match Fit Season 4 Ep 5 {{!}} SPORT {{!}} ThreeNow |url=https://www.threenow.co.nz/shows/match-fit/season-4-ep-5/S2339-580/M88774-671 |access-date=2024-08-25 |website=www.threenow.co.nz}}</ref>
